title: Inventory Demand-Supply Mismatch Detection
dataSources: **Inventory snapshots (authoritative on-hand).** SAP MM `MARD` (storage-location quantities) plus `MARC` (plant-material planning parameters—including default `lead_time_days`, lot sizes relevant for planners) or Oracle Inventory interfaces such as `MTL_ONHAND_QUANTITIES` joined to material master routing lead times via DB Connect scheduled queries (typically daily post-MRP close, optional intraday for A-class SKUs). Land on `index=inventory`, `sourcetype=erp:inventory_snapshot` with **`material_id`, `plant`, `qty_on_hand`, `safety_stock`, `lead_time_days`,** and snapshot versioning fields. **Demand / forecast.** Feed from SAP Integrated Business Planning (`IBP`) / Advanced Planning (`APO`) forecasting tables or Oracle ASCP forecasts, or curated flat-file exports dropped to a watcher path; normalize to **`index=demand`, `sourcetype=forecast:demand`** with **`daily_demand`, `forecast_date`,** and `forecast_batch_id`. **Operational keys:** reconcile UoM and calendar (5-day vs continuous consumption). **Volumes:** midsize discrete manufacturers commonly hold 50K–500K active SKU-plant combinations—pre-aggregate demand with scheduled searches when join fan-out threatens subsearch ceilings.
app: DB Connect 3.x ([Splunkbase 2686](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/2686)) for ERP/MES data extraction. For real-time process data: Splunk Edge Hub with OPC-UA or Modbus TCP sources, or custom scripted inputs polling historian APIs. Lookup files for golden batch profiles managed via KV store or CSV.
spl:
index=inventory sourcetype=erp:inventory_snapshot
| join type=left material_id [
    search index=demand sourcetype=forecast:demand
    | stats avg(daily_demand) as avg_demand, max(daily_demand) as peak_demand by material_id
  ]
| eval days_of_supply=if(avg_demand>0, round(qty_on_hand/avg_demand, 1), 9999),
       reorder_point=round(avg_demand*lead_time_days*1.2, 0)
| eval risk=case(days_of_supply<lead_time_days, "stockout_risk", days_of_supply>lead_time_days*4, "excess", 1=1, "healthy")
| where risk!="healthy"
| sort risk, days_of_supply
description: For each material, the detection joins the latest inventory snapshot to rolling demand averages, computes days-of-cover versus replenishment lead time, labels rows as imminent stock-out risk or excess stocking, and returns only materials that deviate from a healthy coverage band.
value: Planners protect revenue and service levels by adjusting purchase orders and production schedules before a line-down event, while also releasing cash tied up in slow-moving overstocks instead of discovering the problem weeks later in a static spreadsheet.
implementation: Prefer a scheduled summary of `forecast:demand` by `material_id` to shrink the subsearch. Align `lead_time_days` to the active vendor route; buffer factors (here 1.2 on reorder) should be category-specific. **Domain context:** seasonality and promotions can distort `avg_demand`—use the same forecast version operations trusts. **Splunk:** `join` is subsearch-limited; for large catalogs, use `lookup` from a pre-built KV or summary index.
visualization: Dashboard layout — **Row 1:** Panel 1 *Coverage scatter* (`days_of_supply` x-axis vs `lead_time_days` y-axis, point color=`risk`, size=`qty_on_hand`). **Row 2, left:** Panel 2 *Exception table* (`material_id`, `plant`, `qty_on_hand`, `safety_stock`, `avg_demand`, `peak_demand`, `days_of_supply`, `reorder_point`, `risk`, drill-through to PO recommendations). **Row 2, right:** Panel 3 *Plant / buyer stacked column chart* (`count(material)` by plant with stack split `stockout_risk` vs `excess`). **Row 3:** Panel 4 *Single value triage KPI* (`dc(material_id)` in `stockout_risk`, delta vs prior snapshot, sparkline from summary index).

references.title: APICS — Supply Chain Operations Reference (SCOR) Model
Gartner — Supply Chain Risk Management Framework
FDA — Process Validation: General Principles and Practices (Guidance)
Splunk Lantern — Use Case Explorer: Manufacturing

knownFalsePositives: **Seasonal demand shifts.** Promotional builds, OEM model-year changeovers, and agricultural harvesting seasons swing `daily_demand` far above a trailing average, collapsing computed `days_of_supply` and flooding “stockout” queues even though planners already placed blanket POs covering the spike. Mitigation: ingest S&OP `seasonality_index` lookup; classify SKUs into demand pattern families (smooth, intermittent, lumpy); use forecast percentiles (`p90`) instead of `avg` within those families; widen alert horizons during sanctioned campaign windows keyed off marketing calendars. **Supplier M&A and network redesign.** Consolidated plants or cross-dock strategy changes abruptly alter effective `lead_time_days` cached in ERP while material master updates lag—the model compares stale lead times against fresh inventory, mislabeling strategic buffer stock as excess or understating true risk. Mitigation: join latest published **transportation routing** extracts; trigger master-data SLA alerts inside Splunk when `lead_time_days` staleness exceeds 14 days; temporarily freeze mismatch alerts during approved network transformations with ticket IDs in `splunk_audit` KV. **Recipe / BOM version updates.** Engineering revisions swap components or BOM effectivity dates without synchronizing forecasting bills, so `forecast:demand` still references retiring `material_id` generations while inventory tracks successor parts—joined demand understates consumption of on-hand supersession chains. Mitigation: maintain phased `material_supersession.csv` lookups; remap demand using effectivity-aware mapping subsearch prior to mismatch detection; reconcile alerts with ECM change tickets. **Planned maintenance and plant shutdown freezes.** Mandatory holiday shutdown windows zero out outbound consumption in forecasts while physical inventory sits idle—the algorithm sees “infinite cover” tagging healthy SKUs as `excess` without recognizing intentional build-ahead inventory. Mitigation: overlay plant calendar lookup marking non-working intervals; suppress `excess` path when inventory build is tied to validated `campaign_build_tag` fields from MPS. **Raw material variability and phantom ATP.** Allocation or quality holds reduce **available** stock while `qty_on_hand` gross remains optimistic; conversely blocked stock still counts as physically present inflating usable cover in Splunk math if quality status not joined. Mitigation: enrich snapshots with QA `inspection_stock` and ATP views; bifurcate `qty_available` versus `qty_on_hand`; escalate only when unrestricted stock breaches thresholds.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• DB Connect 3.x ([Splunkbase 2686](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/2686)) with scheduled daily (or intraday) access to inventory facts: SAP MM `MARD` (storage location stock) / `MARC` (plant-material status) or Oracle Inventory `MTL_ONHAND_QUANTITIES`, normalized to `index=inventory`, `sourcetype=erp:inventory_snapshot`, including `material_id`, `plant`, `qty_on_hand`, `safety_stock`, `lead_time_days`.
• Demand planning extracts from SAP APO/IBP, Oracle ASCP, or approved flat-file forecast drops into `index=demand`, `sourcetype=forecast:demand` with `daily_demand`, `forecast_date`, and consistent unit of measure with inventory.
• Material status master (active, blocked, NPI) to exclude non-repeatable SKUs from automated alerts; macro parameters for service-level factors (1.2× lead time buffer default).
• Performance architecture decision: if `join` subsearch exceeds limits, precompute `demand_by_material_summary` via scheduled search to KV store or CSV lookup.
• UAT dataset of five materials with known healthy, stockout, and excess states signed by master scheduler.
• Observability of batch jobs: `_internal` or OS logs for DB Connect failures; data quality dashboard for null `lead_time_days`.

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Configure **Inventory snapshots** as a DB Connect batch input running after MRP nightly close (typical 02:00 plant time). SQL should join plant, sloc, and material to output authoritative `qty_on_hand`. Map timestamps to snapshot boundary (`_time` = schedule run). In `props.conf`, set `sourcetype=erp:inventory_snapshot`, define `TIME_FORMAT` for snapshot run, and `EVAL-material_id = upper(material_id)` if plants mix case. **Demand feed:** if IBP exports CSV to share, use monitored batch folder or `batch` input; if database tables exist, second DB Connect input with `forecast_date` filter for rolling 180 days. Ensure `daily_demand` is mean demand per day in selling UoM. For mixed calendar (5-day vs 7-day manufacturing), add `working_day_factor` field from lookup and adjust in macro. **transforms.conf** can compute implied `reorder_point = safety_stock + lead_time_days * daily_demand * service_factor` at index time for simpler searches. Document lag: inventory snapshot must not precede demand file version—version stamp `forecast_id` as field for filtering.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Operational macro `m_inventory_demand_join` wraps the join to ease maintenance:

```spl
index=inventory sourcetype=erp:inventory_snapshot
| join type=left material_id [
    search index=demand sourcetype=forecast:demand
    | stats avg(daily_demand) as avg_demand, max(daily_demand) as peak_demand by material_id
  ]
| eval days_of_supply=if(avg_demand>0, round(qty_on_hand/avg_demand, 1), 9999),
       reorder_point=round(avg_demand*lead_time_days*1.2, 0)
| eval risk=case(days_of_supply<lead_time_days, "stockout_risk", days_of_supply>lead_time_days*4, "excess", 1=1, "healthy")
| where risk!="healthy"
| sort risk, days_of_supply
```

Understanding this SPL
`join` correlates deterministic material keys across indexes—classic bill-of-materials analytics. Splunk joins are row-limited (~50,000 default subsearch results); high-SKU catalogs should externalize the bracketed demand search to `| lookup demand_material_summary material_id OUTPUT avg_demand peak_demand`. `days_of_supply` divides frozen inventory snapshot by smoothed consumption; sentinel `9999` prevents divide-by-zero yet flags planners via `risk=excess` path when interpreted alongside `avg_demand==0`. The `eval risk` harness encodes heuristic guardrails: imminent stock-out when cover falls below deterministic lead horizon; excess above 4× lead-time demand (tunable macro). Statistical nuance—this isn’t classic ARIMA; adding `streamstats` on `qty_on_hand` can compute volatility-adjusted buffers (hybrid Safety Stock z). For SKU-level anomalies vs category baselines, append `eventstats perc90(qty_on_hand) as peer_hi by commodity_class` filtered from CMMD lookup—but keep base SPL lean for alerting.

Schedule as Alert
Cron **15 6 * * *** daily after MRP publications, earliest **-3d@m** to capture weekend lag, alert if **results > threshold** (macro `material_alert_min=25`), throttle **`material_id+plant`** 24h except `stockout_risk` which pages immediately (`alert.suppress.period=0`, route to escalation queue).

Step 3 — Validate
Confirm anomalies against planner-facing **ERP inventory UIs** (SAP MB52/MB51 history, WM transactions if relevant, Oracle Inventory Inquiry) and the operational **forecast UI or published IBP/APO workbook** tied to `forecast_id`.
**(a)**(a)** Compare fifty random rows `qty_on_hand` vs SAP MB52 / ASCP Inquiry. **(b)** Match `avg_demand` to official forecast archive version `forecast_id` stamped last night. **(c)** Walk three `lead_time_days` values back to routed approved vendor—not legacy phantom routes. **(d)** Verify `risk` aligns with planners’ exception report for same day. **(e)** ClosedPlant holiday test: confirm inventory snapshot job skipped with explicit marker so stale data cannot misclassify. **(f)** Subsearch limit test: temporarily raise `subsearch_maxout` in `limits.conf` on sandbox only to confirm join completeness vs lookup migration plan.

Step 4 — Operationalize
**Dashboard:** scatter `days_of_supply` vs `lead_time_days` colored by `risk`; treemap of excess by `plant`; table with `reorder_point`, `peak_demand`; single value `dc(material_id)` in `stockout_risk`. **Alerting:** email buyers with embedded pivot link; ServiceNow incident template `INV-MISMATCH`. **Runbook:** (1) verify forecast version, (2) check inbound PO ASNs, (3) adjust MRP lot size if excess due to MOQ, (4) expedite if stockout and customer critical, (5) log decision in S&OP minutes.

Step 5 — Troubleshooting
**Null `avg_demand` post-join:** demand index sourcetype filter mismatch or `material_id` formatting—use `makeresults` test join. **Everything labelled excess:** unit conversion ounces vs each—consult `uom_lookup`. **Phantom stockout after go-live:** duplicate snapshot rows summed twice—insert `dedup plant material_id`. **Volatile false positives:** seasonality absent—implement `predict`/`lineartrend` ancillary model or switch to Holt-Winters export from planning system. **`join` timeout:** migrate to KV summary. **Timezone skew:** mismatched `_time` on snapshot vs UTC—enforce `TZ` on DB Connect JVM.

Supplementary depth — inventory optimization under real-world planning noise
Extend the mismatch detection program by explicitly modeling **forecast version governance**. Each planning cycle should stamp `forecast_id`, `published_by`, and `horizon_months` onto demand events; without this, comparing Thursday’s inventory snapshot to Tuesday’s obsolete forecast recreates false stockouts at scale. When SAP IBP or Oracle ASCP produces multiple scenarios (baseline vs sales & operations consensus), land the official “operative” scenario only unless your governance requires alerting on divergence between scenarios—which is a distinct advanced UC. Inventory snapshots must carry **inventory status** distinctions: unrestricted, quality inspection, blocked, and in transit if your ERP separates them; alerting on unrestricted stock only aligns with ATP reality planners experience on the ERP screen.

Architecturally, migrate from subsearch joins to **KV lookups** once SKUs exceed low tens of thousands. A nightly saved search aggregates `forecast:demand` to `avg_demand`, `stdev`, `p90`, and `intercept` placeholders for Holt smoothing parameters if you evolve the model; `outputlookup demand_summary_by_material_kv` avoids join timeouts and lets you inspect demand rows independently when debugging. Implement **plants as first-class facets**—either include `plant` in `by` clauses throughout or run parallel searches saved per manufacturing region depending on indexer affinity. Tune service factors using ABC classification: macro `scm_service_factor` pulled from `material_abc_lookup` keyed by cumulative annual usage percent.

Statistical augmentation: optionally compute **coefficient of variation** CV = `stdev(daily_demand)/avg_demand` in the demand pre-summary; high CV intermittent items should downgrade automatic stock-out alerts unless `peak_demand` exceeds a phased threshold indicative of contractual obligations. Discuss with finance how excess inventory aligns to carrying cost KPIs; the dashboard can show estimated dollars at risk using `qty_on_hand * standard_cost` joined from a cost cube extract. Resilience testing should validate behavior when the demand file stalls: create synthetic monitors that alert if no `forecast:demand` events arrive within SLA, preventing silent “everything is healthy” conclusions.

Change management reminders: ERP go-lives remap `material_id`; maintain crosswalk tables for eighteen months minimally. Localization issues—European decimal commas—should be sanitized at index time. Document **runbook interplay** between buyers and planners so stockout alerts trigger MRP rerun requests rather than ad hoc PO duplication. Where appropriate, expose API results to external planning workbenches via CSV export triggered by Splunk orchestration, respecting least privilege credentials.

Secondary supplement — S&OP choreography, CFO metrics, and exception-based automation
Elevate mismatch detection beyond static thresholds by aligning with **Rolling S&OP cadence buckets** (Executive, Demand, Supply, Integrated Reconciliation). Tag inventory snapshots with `sop_week` identifiers from planning calendars so Splunk overlays align with planner reviews; when anomalies appear outside scheduled reconciliation windows, route with higher severity. CFO-facing extensions include translating excess units into carrying cost proxies using weighted average costs and warehousing rates from finance feeds—subject to treasury approval—to prioritize capital release conversations.

Implement **supplier collaboration signals** optionally: inbound ASNs near due dates may downgrade stock-out severity if imminent receipt exists but GR not yet processed; hook optional `asn_expected_qty` lookups from supplier portals. Conversely, flagged customer backorders amplify stock-out severity classification using CRM extracts. Respect **dual units of measure** situations by computing parallel `days_of_supply` per UoM and choosing min cover as conservative signal.

Operational analytics team should orchestrate Splunk alerts with robotic process automation bots that open ephemeral MRP net change requests—not automatic PO placements without human approvals in regulated sectors. Maintain **sandbox indexes** refreshed weekly with masked SKUs enabling training without production noise. Tune join vs lookup rollout using measured p95 latency and indexer load; escalate architecture review crossing two hundred fifty thousand SKUs considering SmartStore vs traditional storage trade-offs.

Conduct semiannual segmentation refresh for ABC classifications and confirm risk macros still align planner tribal knowledge. Embed **education curriculum** bridging APICS body of knowledge concepts with Splunk UI navigation. Cross-pollinate with sustainability teams when excess tied to obsolescence presents circular economy opportunities. Document force majeure annotation paths so acts of nature toggles adjust excess tolerances contextually. Close loop with post-audit CAPA if repeated stockouts trace to systematic lead time master neglect—Splunk evidence accelerates master data governance funding requests.

Tertiary supplement — quantile service targets, planning interoperability, working capital storytelling
Add optional summary searches that compute rolling `p90(daily_demand)` and `stdev(daily_demand)` by `material_id` for service-level targeting where mean-based cover misrepresents skewed consumption (spare parts, intermittency). Document how to swap `avg_demand` for `p90_demand` via macro without rewriting the entire pipeline. Planning interoperability: export daily exception CSV to IBP process orchestration folders or secure SFTP drops used by Kinaxis/o9 middleware—Splunk remains an exception engine, not a planning system of record.

Working capital narration: multiply excess `qty_on_hand` by `standard_cost` from finance extracts joined through `material_id` to estimate tied-up dollars; disclaim GAAP nuances and require CFO sign-off slides. Operational risk: annotate alerts with **customer-criticality flags** sourced from ATP / customer backlog tables where contractually permissible so buyers triage ethically. Incident learning: quarterly reviews comparing near-missed stockouts averted versus alert precision sustain funding. Disaster recovery rehearsals verify demand summary KV regeneration scripts after indexer restores so joins never silently return empties implying false health.

Quaternary supplement — SPL hygiene, indexer sizing, observability loops, stakeholder communications
Operational teams should encapsulate repetitive `eval` normalizations (`supplier_id`, `material_id`) inside **macros** `m_normalize_scm`, `m_normalize_inv` respecting least-privilege edits via `default.meta`. Document **Splunk ingest lag SLO**: if median `_indextime-_time` drifts beyond agreed minutes, degrade alert automation to investigative-only mode until ingestion recovers—embed this policy in Incident Response playbooks bridging ITOps and analytics owners. Estimate daily indexed volume from DB Connect PO line widths (bytes/row × rows/day) multiplied by archival retention to avoid surprise license overrun; revisit SmartStore ratios quarterly.

Maintain **Synthetic event canaries**: lightweight scheduled searches injecting tagged harmless events validating index routing end-to-end. Training communications should include glossary bridging business concepts (tier, BOM, lapse) versus Splunk field names minimizing confusion escalation noise. Celebrate wins publicly when analytics prevented outages or leakage—cultural reinforcement strengthens continuous funding. Periodic third-party Red Team review of alert logic ensures thresholds remain defensible under adversarial gaming (suppliers gaming on-time metrics with early premature GR postings, etc.). Finally, align documentation with corporate knowledge base standards (Confluence / SharePoint) including ownership matrix, review cadence, retirement criteria for deprecated macros—good lifecycle hygiene prevents stale guidance misdirecting new analysts during attrition transitions commonly experienced dynamic industries.

Warehousing, logistics, and SIOP alignment
Explain how optional future feeds from WMS pick transactions or TMS carrier ETAs can disambiguate planning noise from physical misplaced inventory—but only integrate when OT/IT policy permits. Tie alert publication cadence to SIOP calendars so planners review Splunk exceptions when decision rights and executive sponsors convene—not during unrelated silo meetings stranded without authority. Embed optional CO2/obsolescence commentary on excess outliers for sustainability councils without claiming precision beyond materially accurate estimates sanctioned by controllers.

Platform operations and continuous improvement (cross-cutting)
Maintain version-controlled exports of saved search definitions or Splunk ACS app bundles so promotions from development to production stay diff-reviewed. Map service accounts to LDAP-backed Splunk roles that separate macro authors from analysts who run searches read-only on production. Track scheduler duration, skipped search counts, and search peer saturation on an internal observability dashboard; degradations frequently foreshadow muted alerts. Coordinate vulnerability management for DB Connect, Edge Hub forwarders, and HEC endpoints with cybersecurity patch cycles, followed by scripted regression reruns using frozen time windows and canned queries. Establish legal hold-aware retention discussions with records management before litigation freezes supplier or claims evidence; know whether to pause bucket rolling deletion. Run quarterly cross-training so backup owners can execute runbooks. Auto-open tickets with SPL excerpts, alert titles, and deep links. Review alert precision with labeled outcomes monthly. Partner with FinOps on license efficiency—summary indexing and proper source typing reduce cost per insight. Forward critical `scheduler` and `deployer` messages to centralized monitoring so the monitoring platform itself stays observable. These habits stabilize analytics programs irrespective of vertical specifics.

Sixth supplement — advanced SPL patterns, probabilistic forecasting handoffs, catalog governance
Introduce cohort macros grouping materials by replenishment policies (MRP-type, reorder point, Kanban supermarket) because identical numeric thresholds seldom suit heterogeneous planning strategies. Outline optional appendix searches computing target stock as `norm_inv_service_level*stdev_demand*sqrt(lead_time)` when demand and lead time variance tables exist—keep core alert intentionally simpler but document extension path. Describe catalog governance linking Splunk exception exports to PIM/MDM cleanup queues when duplicate `material_id` aliases appear. Emphasize collaboration with internal audit on segregation of duties: who may alter lead times in ERP versus who approves Splunk macro promotions should differ. Close with reminder that physical cycle count programs remain primary ground truth—Splunk highlights statistical suspects for cycle count targeting, not replacing wall-to-wall inventory policies.

Additional depth — performance, security, and multilingual planning operations
Profile the join-based search under load; if p95 latency exceeds planner patience during morning peak, pre-materialize demand aggregates and switch the join to `lookup` immediately. Harden DB Connect connections with least privilege, application intent logging, and JDBC URL parameters tuned for read consistency (avoid dirty reads that mix partially posted MRP results). For multinational rollouts, localize alert email templates and document unit-of-measure conversion tables per country site. Maintain an index exclusion for test plants so sandbox PO movements never pollute executive supply risk maps accidentally. Rotate HEC tokens used for optional logistics enrichments proactively. Partner with cybersecurity for periodic ATT&CK-style tabletop exercises probing whether fabricated supplier score events could spoof sourcing decisions—and implement signing or allow-listed source IP mitigations accordingly.

Closing commitments — stewardship and iterative tuning
Establish an annual threshold review aligning macro multipliers (`excess_multiple`, service factor 1.2 baseline) with audited inventory turnover KPIs and customer fill-rate contractual SLAs. Record each Splunk-side change with approvers, CAB ticket IDs, and rollback instructions. Maintain week-over-week control charts on false positives and integrate DMAIC cycles when systemic noise appears. Celebrate cross-site knowledge shares so regional planners interpret identical SPL outputs consistently.

Final notes — consignment and vendor-managed inventory overlays
Differentiate ownership models: consigned inventory at customer sites requires optional `ownership_flag` ingestion so mismatch logic references economically relevant stock versus physically located stock ambiguously overstating corporate exposure otherwise.
grandmaExplanation: We compare what is on the shelf with what we expect to sell, using how long new stock takes to arrive. When we are about to run out—or we are sitting on too much—we raise a hand before machines go quiet or money stays locked in boxes.
